docs.title: MkDocs Starter Kit
docs.text: This starter kit is based on the Material theme for MkDocs, a static site generator built for documentation.

docs.title: Manual
docs.text:
1. Ensure that you're running Node >= 8.11.2

  2. Run npm install to install the Markdownlint dependencies

  3. Make sure you have a recent version of Python - Python version >= 3.6

  4. Create a Python virtual environment and install the dependencies Install if you wish to build and serve your doc locally

    Or use this snippet to setup mkdocs:

    1. Create virtual environment with a folder env

          python3 -m venv env

    2. Activate virtual environment

      Each time the terminal is opened you need to activate the virtual environment.

      • Windows:

      .\env\Scripts\activate

      • Linux:

      source env/bin/activate

    3. Install the requirements

      pip install -r requirements.txt

    4. Start the mkdocs

      mkdocs serve

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• npm run lint: Runs Markdownlint-cli and outputs linting errors to the console.
  • npm run format: Run Markdownlint-cli and fix the linting errors
  • npm run prepare: Run to have Git hooks
  • npm start: Builds and serves the MkDocs site to localhost:8000, auto-refreshes on file change.
  • npm build: Builds the MkDocs site to site/ folder. There's no need to build the site before committing though -- we handle this in the CI defined in the .gitlab-ci.yml file.

Linting rules

We've configured Markdownlint to use the "Relaxed Rules" by default. Refer to the full list of rules if you wish to add or modify your linting rules. To save time, we recommend that you add Markdownlint to your IDE: Sublime | VSCode.

docs.title: Extensions
docs.text:
We've enabled several extensions in the mkdocs.yml file, notably:

  • Admonition for styled blocks (e.g. info, warning, bug, etc)
  • CodeHilite for highlighting syntax in code blocks
  • Permalinks for linking to subsections of the doc

If you wish to add an extension, head to the Extensions section in the Material for MkDocs documentation to see which are available.

docs.title: Plugins
docs.text:
We've also enabled some plugins in the mkdocs.yml file, notably:

  • Search for searching through your doc using lunr.js
  • Revision date for outputting the git revision date of the corresponding markdown file at the bottom of the page
  • Awesome Pages Plugin an mkdocs plugin that simplifies configuring page titles and their order
  • Minify for html/css/js minification
  • Redirects to create page redirects (e.g. for moved/renamed pages)

If you wish to add a plugin, head to the Setup section in the Material for MkDocs documentation to see which are available.

docs.title: Azure Policy
docs.text: The Organization Policy Service gives you centralized and programmatic control over our GCP organization's cloud resources. Public Cloud team and Security Architect Cloud Security will be able to configure constraints across our entire FWH resource hierarchy.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Centralize control to configure restrictions on how your organization’s resources can be used.
  • Define and establish guardrails for your development teams to stay within compliance boundaries.
  • Help project owners and their teams move quickly without worry of breaking compliance.

docs.title: Organization Policies in place at FWH
docs.text:
IMPORTANT: FWH GCP Organization Policies

The organization policies below should never be changed without the approval of Security Architect Cloud Security Architects.

docs.title: Domain restricted sharing
docs.text:
Information Description Policy name Domain restricted sharing Description This list constraint defines one or more Cloud Identity or Google Workspace customer IDs whose principals can be added to IAM policies. By default, all user identities are allowed to be added to IAM policies. Only allowed values can be defined in this constraint, denied values are not supported. If this constraint is active, only principals that belong to the allowed customer IDs can be added to IAM policies. Applies to Inheritance from Organization Level "fwh.com" Effective Policy Allowed : C02i8q32t Domains Accepted fwh.com (Primary Domain), ivory-tower.fr (Secondary Domain), massive.se (Secondary Domain), redstorm.com (Secondary Domain)

IMPORTANT: FWH GCP Organization Policies

The organization policies below should never be changed without the approval of Security Architect Cloud Security Architects.

docs.title: Skip Default Network Creation
docs.text: Information Description Policy name Skip default network creation Description This boolean constraint skips the creation of the default network and related resources during Google Cloud Platform Project resource creation where this constraint is set to True. By default, a default network and supporting resources are automatically created when creating a Project resource. Applies to Inheritance from Organization Level "fwh.com" Effective Policy Enforcement : Enforced

docs.title: Disable service account key creation
docs.text:
Information Description Policy name Disable service account key creation Description This boolean constraint disables the creation of service account external keys where this constraint is set to "True". By default, service account external keys can be created by users based on their Cloud IAM roles and permissions. Applies to Inheritance from Organization Level "fwh.com" Effective Policy Enforcement : Enforced

docs.title: Azure
docs.text: Welcome to the Azure section. Add your Azure-related documentation here.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Azure Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) is a comprehensive guide that helps organizations develop a structured approach to cloud adoption. It provides a proven methodology to align business, people, and technology through strategic planning and execution.

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text:
• Strategy - Define your cloud adoption goals and business drivers
  • Plan - Assess your current state and create adoption roadmaps
  • Ready - Prepare your environment for cloud migration
  • Adopt - Execute migration and innovation initiatives
  • Govern - Implement policies and compliance controls
  • Manage - Establish operational management practices

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Reduced risk and faster time to value
  • Clear governance and compliance frameworks
  • Aligned organizational structure and skills
  • Cost optimization and resource efficiency
  • Scalable and repeatable processes

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Explore the CAF framework phases to understand how to structure your cloud adoption journey and maximize business value.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: An Azure Landing Zone is a pre-configured environment that provides the foundation for your cloud workloads. It implements best practices for security, governance, networking, and operations, enabling you to deploy applications quickly while maintaining compliance and control.

docs.title: Key Characteristics
docs.text:
• Pre-configured governance - Policies and compliance controls are built-in
  • Secure by default - Network isolation, encryption, and identity management
  • Scalable foundation - Designed to grow with your organization
  • Operational readiness - Monitoring, logging, and management tools included
  • Cost management - Billing and cost optimization guardrails

docs.title: Core Components
docs.text:
• Identity and Access Management - Azure AD integration and role-based access control
  • Network Architecture - Virtual networks, subnets, and connectivity patterns
  • Governance - Management groups, policies, and compliance frameworks
  • Security - Firewalls, threat protection, and compliance monitoring
  • Operations - Logging, monitoring, and alert management

docs.title: Implementation Approach
docs.text: Landing Zones can be deployed using Infrastructure as Code (Terraform, Bicep) for consistency and repeatability across your cloud environment.

docs.title: Security Layers
docs.text:
Application Layer
              ↓
      Web Application Firewall (WAF)
              ↓
      Network Security Groups (NSGs)
              ↓
      Azure Firewall / NVA
              ↓
      DDoS Protection
              ↓
      Encryption in Transit & At Rest

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Subscriptions as Scale Unit - Use multiple subscriptions for isolation
  2. Management Groups for Governance - Apply policies at scale
  3. Hub-and-Spoke Network - Centralized security and connectivity
  4. Separation of Duties - Different subscriptions for different purposes
  5. Centralized Logging - All logs flow to central location

docs.title: Corp Landing Zone
docs.text:
• For regulated workloads
  • Requires compliance controls
  • Connected to on-premises via ExpressRoute
  • Example: Financial systems, healthcare

docs.title: Online Landing Zone
docs.text:
• For internet-facing applications
  • May have less strict governance
  • Cloud-native architectures
  • Example: Public web apps, SaaS applications

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Enterprise-Scale Architecture
  • Network Topology and Connectivity
  • Azure Landing Zone Design Guide

docs.title: What is a Landing Zone?
docs.text: A Landing Zone is a pre-provisioned Azure environment that provides a foundation for deploying cloud workloads. It implements cloud best practices for security, governance, networking, and operations from day one.

docs.title: 1. Foundation First
docs.text: Building a solid foundation before deploying workloads reduces rework and technical debt.

docs.title: 2. Security by Default
docs.text: Landing zones implement security controls at multiple layers rather than adding them later.

docs.title: 3. Governance at Scale
docs.text: Policies and controls are centralized, enabling consistent compliance across the organization.

docs.title: 4. Operational Readiness
docs.text: Monitoring, logging, and management capabilities are built-in, not added as an afterthought.

docs.title: Why You Need a Landing Zone
docs.text: Challenge Landing Zone Solution Security gaps in ad-hoc deployments Pre-configured security controls Lack of governance Built-in policy enforcement Operational blindness Integrated monitoring and logging Cost overruns Budget controls and cost optimization Compliance violations Pre-configured compliance guardrails

docs.title: Landing Zone vs. Basic Environment
docs.text:
BASIC ENVIRONMENT          LANDING ZONE
├── Subscriptions         ├── Subscriptions
├── Resource Groups       ├── Management Groups
└── Resources            ├── Resource Groups
                         ├── Governance Policies
                         ├── Security Controls
                         ├── Networking
                         ├── Monitoring
                         ├── Compliance
                         └── Cost Management

docs.title: Real-World Example
docs.text:
Without Landing Zone: - Deploy VM → realize no logging configured → add logging later - Deploy database → realize no backup policy → add policy later - Deploy web app → realize no WAF → add WAF later

With Landing Zone: - All logging, backup, and WAF policies already in place - Deploy immediately with confidence - Focus on business value, not infrastructure fixes

docs.text:

  • Enterprise Scale Landing Zone
  • CAF Ready Phase

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Governance in landing zones ensures consistent policies, compliance, and cost control across all cloud resources through a centralized approach.

docs.title: Azure Policy Framework
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          AZURE POLICY SCOPE              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Policy Definition                        │
│  └─ What is allowed/denied               │
│                                          │
│ Policy Assignment                        │
│  └─ Where to apply (MG, Sub, RG)        │
│                                          │
│ Compliance Evaluation                    │
│  └─ Check if resources are compliant    │
│                                          │
│ Remediation                              │
│  └─ Auto-fix non-compliant resources    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Common Landing Zone Policies
docs.text: Policy Purpose Example Location Restriction Limit where resources can be created Only East US, West US SKU Restriction Control resource sizes No expensive VMs Tagging Requirements Enforce resource organization Require Cost Center tag Encryption Require data protection Enable disk encryption Logging Enable audit trail Send logs to Log Analytics Network Access Control connectivity No public IPs

docs.title: Management Groups
docs.text:
Management Groups provide:
├── Policy Inheritance
├── Role Assignment at Scale
├── Centralized Governance
├── Organization Alignment
└── Cost Analysis

docs.title: Cost Control Mechanisms
docs.text:
1. BUDGET ALERTS
   └─ Notify when spending exceeds threshold

2. SPENDING LIMITS
   └─ Prevent exceeding allocated budget

3. RESERVATION RECOMMENDATIONS
   └─ Purchase 1-3 year commitments

4. COST ALLOCATION
   └─ Tag and track costs by department

5. POLICY-BASED RESTRICTIONS
   └─ Prevent expensive resources

docs.title: Built-in Compliance Standards
docs.text:
• CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations Benchmark - Cloud security baseline
  • HIPAA/HITRUST - Healthcare compliance
  • PCI DSS - Payment card security
  • SOC 2 - Service organization controls
  • ISO 27001 - Information security management

docs.title: Audit Trail
docs.text:
All actions are logged: 

Who did what → When → Where → Why
User/Service → Action → Timestamp → Resource → Status

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Identity and Access Management (IAM) is the foundation of landing zone security. It controls who can access what resources and what actions they can perform.

docs.title: Azure Active Directory (Azure AD)
docs.text:
• Central identity provider for your organization
  • Manages users, groups, and application permissions
  • Enables single sign-on (SSO) across cloud and on-premises

docs.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
docs.text:
• Assign roles to users, groups, and service principals
  • Control resource access at subscription and resource group level
  • Built-in roles: Owner, Contributor, Reader, etc.

docs.title: 1. Use Groups for Access Management
docs.text:
Instead of:  User → Direct Role Assignment

Better:      User → Group → Role Assignment
             (Easier to manage at scale)

docs.title: 2. Apply Least Privilege
docs.text:
• Assign minimum permissions needed
  • Review and audit regularly
  • Remove unnecessary access

docs.title: 3. Enable Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
docs.text:
• Require second authentication factor
  • Protects against compromised passwords
  • Essential for privileged accounts

docs.title: 4. Use Managed Identities
docs.text:
• For applications and services
  • Eliminates need for password management
  • Automatic credential rotation

docs.title: RBAC Best Practices
docs.text: Do Don't Use groups for role assignment Assign roles directly to users Use built-in roles when possible Create custom roles unless necessary Use service principals for apps Use user accounts for applications Enable MFA for privileged users Allow password-only authentication Review permissions quarterly Set and forget

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Azure AD Documentation
  • RBAC Documentation
  • Best Practices for Azure AD
  • Managed Identities

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Network architecture is critical in landing zones. It provides connectivity, security boundaries, and centralized management for all cloud resources.

docs.title: 1. Virtual Network (VNet)
docs.text:
• Isolated network space in Azure
  • Contains subnets for resource organization
  • Supports hybrid connectivity

docs.title: 2. Subnets
docs.text:
• Network segments within a VNet
  • Can have Network Security Groups (NSGs)
  • /24 or /25 typically recommended

docs.title: 3. Network Security Groups (NSGs)
docs.text:
• Firewall rules at subnet and NIC level
  • Inbound and outbound rules
  • Allow/Deny traffic based on protocols and ports

docs.title: 4. Route Tables
docs.text:
• Define how traffic flows
  • Can force traffic through firewall
  • Support custom routing

docs.title: 5. Virtual Network Peering
docs.text:
• Connect VNets together
  • Low-latency connectivity
  • Works across regions and subscriptions

docs.title: Connectivity Options
docs.text:
CONNECTIVITY OPTIONS
                           │
           ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐
           │               │               │
      Site-to-Site      ExpressRoute    Point-to-Site
      VPN                                  VPN
           │               │               │
      - Internet      - Private         - Individual
      - Encrypted     - Dedicated       - Encrypted
      - Lower Cost    - Higher Speed    - Low Cost

docs.text: Control Purpose NSGs Subnet-level filtering Azure Firewall Centralized inspection WAF Application layer protection DDoS Protection Distributed denial of service Private Endpoints Private access to services Service Endpoints Restrict to VNets
